GenPref × Signed Gap & Preference — Results Only (No Refitting)

2026-03-17

Overview

The central question of this analysis is whether a model’s generic preference for one word over another — independent of its corpus-level frequency — shapes how language models represent those words over the course of training. We examine two outcome variables: (1) signed gap, the deviation of a model’s predicted rating difference from what corpus frequency alone would predict, and (2) raw preference, the model’s predicted rating difference without that correction. For each outcome, we run three analyses that approach the training dynamics question in complementary ways.

All models were fit with brms using Bayesian linear mixed-effects regression. Fixed effects include GenPref (genpref_c, centered), relative corpus frequency (freq_prob_c), and overall exposure (log_total_c, log total times the model saw either item, z-scored). Random intercepts are included for item pairs (binom). This document loads pre-fitted models from disk and renders results; no refitting is performed.

Both freq_prob_c and log_total_c are derived from per-checkpoint exposure counts in the training data: alpha_seen and beta_seen record the cumulative number of times \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) have appeared in the training corpus up to that specific checkpoint. Relative corpus frequency is computed as \(\text{freq\_prob\_c} = \text{logistic}(\ln(\alpha_\text{seen} / \beta_\text{seen})) - 0.5\), and overall exposure as \(\text{log\_total\_c} = \log(\alpha_\text{seen} + \beta_\text{seen})\), z-scored. This means both predictors are checkpoint-specific: they reflect the model’s actual training exposure at that point in time rather than fixed whole-corpus statistics. In practice, because training data is drawn from a fixed corpus distribution, the ratio \(\alpha_\text{seen} / \beta_\text{seen}\) should be approximately stable across checkpoints while the total grows monotonically — which is the main reason log_total_c is z-scored within each checkpoint rather than globally (see Analysis 2).

Outcome variables

  • Signed gap (\(\text{preference} - \log\text{FreqRatio}\)): The degree to which the model’s preference for \(\alpha\) over \(\beta\) exceeds what the corpus frequency ratio would predict. A value of zero means the model’s preference exactly matches corpus statistics; positive values indicate the model favors \(\alpha\) more than frequency alone would predict, and negative values indicate less than expected.

  • Preference: The model’s raw rating difference for \(\alpha\) vs. \(\beta\), without correcting for corpus frequency. Because frequency is included as a predictor rather than subtracted from the outcome, this analysis asks what drives preference directly, rather than what drives the residual after frequency is removed.

Analysis structure

Each outcome is examined in three ways. First, we fit a single model at the final training checkpoint to get an overall picture of which predictors matter at the end of training (Analyses 1 and 4). Second, we fit separate models at 20 evenly-spaced checkpoints to track how coefficients evolve over training (Analyses 2 and 5). Third, we include checkpoint itself as a continuous predictor in a single model, which estimates how the relationship between each predictor and the outcome changes with training stage (Analyses 3 and 6).

## Loading required package: DBI
## TRAJ_META: 6 models, 120 checkpoint rows total

## 
## === Trajectory fits ===
## sg_traj      : 120 file(s)
## pref_traj    : 120 file(s)

Descriptive: Signed Gap Over Training

Before examining the regression results, it is useful to look at the signed gap variable itself, aggregated across items at each sampled checkpoint. This gives a purely descriptive sense of how the outcome evolves over training, without any conditioning on the predictors. Two summaries are of interest. The mean signed gap tells us whether, on average, models systematically deviate from corpus-predicted preferences in a consistent direction — whether they tend to favor \(\alpha\) or \(\beta\) more than frequency would predict, and how that tendency develops with more training. The mean absolute signed gap abstracts away from direction and asks a simpler question: are models becoming more decisive over training, assigning larger rating differences regardless of which word they favor?

Note that a mean signed gap of exactly zero would indicate that, averaged across items, models’ preferences align perfectly with corpus statistics. We would not necessarily expect this, since signed gap captures a combination of how well the model has learned frequency information and how its internal representations weight other item properties.

## Descriptive load: sg_traj_znhoughton_opt_c4_350m_seed964_ck20.rds

Plot D1: Mean Signed Gap

Average signed gap across all items at each checkpoint. Shows whether models develop a directional bias (toward alpha or beta) over training and whether that bias is stable, grows, or reverses.

Plot D2: Mean Absolute Signed Gap

Average of |signed gap| at each checkpoint. Shows whether models become more decisive (larger gaps) or more uncertain (gaps shrinking toward zero) as training progresses, regardless of direction.

Plot D3: Preference–Frequency Correlation Over Training

Pearson correlation between the model’s raw preference and \(\log(\alpha_\text{seen}/\beta_\text{seen})\) across items at each checkpoint. This is the most direct measure of how well the model has learned to track corpus frequency: a correlation of 1 would mean the model’s preferences are a perfect linear function of the frequency ratio. Note that this is distinct from the absolute signed gap (Plot D2) — a model could have a high frequency-tracking correlation but still have a large absolute signed gap if it is consistently more extreme than the frequency ratio (i.e., it is on the right scale but amplified). preference and log_freq_ratio are recovered from the stored fit$data columns: log_freq_ratio = \text{logit}(\text{freq\_prob\_c} + 0.5) and preference = \text{signed\_gap} + \text{log\_freq\_ratio}.

Part I — Signed Gap

The signed gap (\(\text{preference} - \log\text{FreqRatio}\)) is the primary outcome of interest. Because it subtracts the corpus frequency baseline, a positive coefficient for any predictor tells us that predictor is associated with the model preferring \(\alpha\) more than frequency alone would predict. This is the cleanest test of whether something beyond frequency is shaping the model’s representations.

Analysis 1: Signed Gap — Final Checkpoint

The simplest starting point is to ask whether any of our predictors explain signed gap at the end of training, treating the model as it exists at its final checkpoint. The model is:

\[\text{signed\_gap} \sim \text{genpref\_c} \times \text{log\_total\_c} + \text{freq\_prob\_c} \times \text{log\_total\_c} + (1 \mid \text{binom})\]

The two-way interactions with log_total_c allow the effects of GenPref and relative frequency to vary depending on how much total exposure the model has had to a given item pair. This matters because we might expect frequency-based effects to be stronger for items that have been seen more, and similarly for GenPref if it is learned gradually through exposure. Asterisks mark credible intervals that exclude zero.

## === Predictor ranges ===

## 13 znhoughton_opt_c4_125m_seed964       GenPref  28407 -0.417  0.003 0.006 0.39

## 16 znhoughton_opt_c4_350m_seed964       GenPref  28407 -0.417  0.003 0.006 0.39 
## 17 znhoughton_opt_c4_350m_seed964       RelFreq… 28407 -0.499  0.005 0.014 0.499
## 18 znhoughton_opt_c4_350m_seed964       Overall… 28407 -2.17  -0.002 0     2.82

Analysis 2: Signed Gap — Per-Checkpoint Trajectory

Analysis 1 tells us about the end state of training, but not about how effects develop. To track the trajectory of each predictor’s influence, we fit the same model separately at 20 evenly-spaced checkpoints across each model’s training run. Each checkpoint gets its own brms fit, producing a separate set of coefficients that can then be plotted as a function of training progress (in tokens).

One key decision is how to handle log_total_c — the log of the total number of times a given item pair has been seen by the model at that checkpoint. Because this quantity grows monotonically across training, z-scoring it globally (across all checkpoints) would confound item-level variation (how much one pair was seen relative to others) with training-stage variation (how deep into training the checkpoint is). Instead, log_total_c is z-scored within each checkpoint’s items, so it captures only between-item differences in exposure at that point in training. This ensures that the coefficient for log_total_c reflects item-level frequency effects rather than a proxy for training stage.

The 20 checkpoints are selected by evenly spacing indices across the full set of available checkpoints for each model, so the x-axis (training tokens) is approximately but not exactly evenly spaced on a linear scale. Plot 2a shows item coverage and predictor distributions across checkpoints as a sanity check. Plot 2b shows the coefficient trajectories. Plot 2c focuses on GenPref specifically, showing the posterior probability that its coefficient is positive at each checkpoint.

Plot 2a: Predictor Statistics Over Training

Plot 2b: Coefficients Over Training

Plot 2c: GenPref Significance Over Training

Analysis 3: Signed Gap — Checkpoint-Interacted

Analysis 2 fits a separate model at each checkpoint, which gives flexible coefficient trajectories but treats each checkpoint as an independent data point. An alternative is to pool all checkpoints into a single model and include training stage as a continuous predictor (tokens_c, z-scored within model). The model is:

\[\text{signed\_gap} \sim (\text{genpref\_c} + \text{freq\_prob\_c} + \text{log\_total\_c}) \times \text{tokens\_c} + (1 \mid \text{binom})\]

The interactions with tokens_c directly estimate whether each predictor’s effect increases or decreases over the course of training. A positive GenPref × Checkpoint interaction, for instance, would indicate that the model’s sensitivity to human generic preferences grows with training. The main effect of tokens_c captures overall drift in signed gap across training independent of the predictors.

The tradeoff relative to Analysis 2 is that this approach assumes each predictor’s effect changes linearly with training stage. The trajectory approach (Analysis 2) is more flexible — it can detect nonlinear trajectories — but loses the ability to directly estimate and test the interaction with training stage in a single model. The marginal effects plots (Plot 3b) show predicted signed gap as a function of training for low, neutral, and high values of each predictor.

Plot 3a: Coefficients

## === Predictor ranges ===

## 21 znhoughton_opt_c4_350m_seed964      GenPref  513632 -0.417  0.005 0.007 0.39 
## 22 znhoughton_opt_c4_350m_seed964      RelFreq… 513632 -0.5    0     0.011 0.5  
## 23 znhoughton_opt_c4_350m_seed964      Overall… 513632 -1.89  -0.052 0     3.17

Plot 3b: Marginal Effects Over Training

Part II — Preference

Analyses 4–6 mirror Analyses 1–3 but use raw preference as the outcome rather than signed gap. Whereas signed gap isolates the portion of the model’s preference that goes beyond what corpus frequency predicts, raw preference conflates frequency-driven and frequency-independent effects. Including freq_prob_c as a predictor in the preference models controls for relative frequency statistically rather than subtracting it from the outcome, which makes the interpretation of the GenPref coefficient subtly different: here, it reflects the effect of GenPref on preference holding relative frequency constant, rather than the effect on the frequency-corrected residual.

The two outcome measures are therefore complementary. If GenPref predicts signed gap, it means generic human preferences shape the model’s representations above and beyond what frequency can explain. If GenPref additionally predicts raw preference, it may be partly because GenPref and relative corpus frequency are correlated — words that humans generically prefer may also tend to appear more often in the corpus. Comparing the two sets of results helps to disentangle these possibilities.

Analysis 4: Preference — Final Checkpoint

Same model structure as Analysis 1, with raw preference as the outcome:

\[\text{preference} \sim \text{genpref\_c} \times \text{log\_total\_c} + \text{freq\_prob\_c} \times \text{log\_total\_c} + (1 \mid \text{binom})\]

## === Predictor ranges ===
## # A tibble: 18 × 7
##    model                                variable     n    min median  mean   max
##    <chr>                                <chr>    <int>  <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>

## 12 znhoughton_opt_c4_1_3b_seed964       Overall… 28407 -2.17  -0.002 0     2.82 
## 13 znhoughton_opt_c4_125m_seed964       GenPref  28407 -0.417  0.003 0.006 0.39 
## 14 znhoughton_opt_c4_125m_seed964       RelFreq… 28407 -0.499  0.005 0.014 0.499
## 15 znhoughton_opt_c4_125m_seed964       Overall… 28407 -2.17  -0.002 0     2.82 
## 16 znhoughton_opt_c4_350m_seed964       GenPref  28407 -0.417  0.003 0.006 0.39 
## 17 znhoughton_opt_c4_350m_seed964       RelFreq… 28407 -0.499  0.005 0.014 0.499
## 18 znhoughton_opt_c4_350m_seed964       Overall… 28407 -2.17  -0.002 0     2.82

Analysis 5: Preference — Per-Checkpoint Trajectory

Same approach as Analysis 2, applied to raw preference. As before, log_total_c is z-scored within each checkpoint’s items to isolate between-item exposure differences from training-stage variation. One thing to bear in mind when comparing these trajectories to those in Analysis 2 is that signed gap has already removed the frequency baseline from the outcome, so any effect of freq_prob_c on signed gap reflects additional frequency sensitivity beyond the baseline correction. In the preference models, by contrast, freq_prob_c is doing more work as a predictor because the outcome still contains the frequency signal.

Plot 5a: Predictor Statistics Over Training

Plot 5b: Coefficients Over Training

Plot 5c: GenPref Significance Over Training

Analysis 6: Preference — Checkpoint-Interacted

Same approach as Analysis 3, applied to raw preference. The model pools all checkpoints and estimates how each predictor’s effect on preference changes linearly with training stage:

\[\text{preference} \sim (\text{genpref\_c} + \text{freq\_prob\_c} + \text{log\_total\_c}) \times \text{tokens\_c} + (1 \mid \text{binom})\]

The marginal effects in Plot 6b are particularly useful for visualizing whether the model’s sensitivity to GenPref at different training stages is practically meaningful in terms of predicted preference, rather than just statistically detectable.

Plot 6a: Coefficients
